title: Wyoming
text:
622
37%
7
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Wyoming, sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence, with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days per month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.¹







In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 63 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 2 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 78 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 89 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (34%) and drug (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (90 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 90 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 1 percent, changing from 2,112 in 2010 to 2,123 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 76 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 89 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 24 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses.















In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 27 percent, dropping from 877 in 2014 to 1,110 in 2020.









In 2020, 53 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 5 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 67 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 73 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 81 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 38 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 34 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for drug offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 5.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 0.5 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Black people spent 8 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.7 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Wyoming.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 3 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 4.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.4 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Wyoming.














Race and EthnicitySex


No disparities compared to White people found for Wyoming.

This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity observed was for robbery offenses, where people of Black, non-Hispanic had 5 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to people of Other race(s) people, who had the shortest time in prison past parole eligibility for these offenses.










Females spent an average of 0.5 fewer years in prison past parole eligibility compared to males.

Avg. Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility 



1.3 Years


0.8 Years


Male


Female



This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 3.8 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to females.


















While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.









¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Wyoming (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/wyoming_doi_report_8_4_22.pdf.² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., “National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.³ Kevin Reitz et al., “American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report” (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, pp. 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, “National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.[pending]
Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program.

text:

People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Utah, sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.¹







In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 66 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 3 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 63 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 70 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 30 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (31%) and property (12%) offenses.















In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (11 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 11 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 13 percent, changing from 6,807 in 2010 to 5,911 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 60 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 93 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 40 percent, dropping from 2,972 in 2014 to 4,156 in 2020.









In 2020, 48 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 8 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 85 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 65 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 86 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 36 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 25 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for property offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 8.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex










This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among negligent manslaughter offenses, where Black people spent 13.5 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1 year fewer on average in prison compared to males in Utah.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 12.1 more years in prison on average compared to females.

In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 2.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah.

title: Utah
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Utah, sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.¹

title: Utah
text:
In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 66 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 3 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 63 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 70 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 30 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (31%) and property (12%) offenses.















In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: Utah
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (11 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 11 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Utah
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Tennessee, sentences under two years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. There is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30–60 percent of the maximum sentence) for parole-release eligibility, differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021, there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses.¹







In 2020, 63 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 29 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 6 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 55 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 49 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (22%) and property (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (60 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 60 for those whose most serious offense is violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 20 percent, changing from 27,451 in 2010 to 21,995 in 2022.

In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 15 percent, dropping from 17,098 in 2014 to 14,517 in 2020.









In 2020, 42 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 1 percent decrease compared to 2014.

title: Tennessee
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: South Carolina
text:
6,585
50%
7
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Carolina, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release at 25 or 33 percent of the maximum sentence with the possibility of somewhat earlier eligibility through credit earnings of 20 days per month. “No parole” crimes are not eligible for parole until they have served 85 percent of the judicial sentence, with three days of credit per month.¹







In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 44 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 2 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 64 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 96 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 49 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (23%) and drug (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 57 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 33 percent, changing from 23,578 in 2010 to 15,759 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 60 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 93 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 21 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses.















In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 30 percent, dropping from 9,409 in 2014 to 6,587 in 2020.









In 2020, 42 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 4 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 65 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 49 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 88 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 32 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for property offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 4.4 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in South Carolina.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 1.2 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 1.2 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among negligent manslaughter offenses, where Hispanic people spent 8.1 more years in prison on average compared to Black people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1.4 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in South Carolina.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 8.1 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 5.3 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in South Carolina.














Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent an average of 1 more years in prison past parole eligibility and Hispanic people spent an average of 0.2 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Pennsylvania judges have discretion to set the durations of minimum terms to parole-release eligibility, which can be any length up to 50 percent of the maximum.¹

title: Pennsylvania
text:
In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 47 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 7 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 47 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 96 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 38 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (23%) and public order (15%) offenses.















In 2020, 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: Pennsylvania
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In North Dakota, most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release soon after they enter prison. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Violent offenses are subject to an 85-percent eligibility rule.¹







In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 37 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 1 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 60 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 85 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 43 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 67 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (36%) and other violent offenses (22%) offenses.















In 2020, 42 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 14 percent, changing from 1,487 in 2010 to 1,689 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 61 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 87 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 38 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 24 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drug offenses.















In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 6 percent, dropping from 1,235 in 2014 to 1,312 in 2020.









In 2020, 57 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 7 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 92 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

In 2020, 81 percent of people released from prison were male.

In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 2.4 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in North Dakota.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 0.1 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent 3.3 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.5 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in North Dakota.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 4.8 more years in prison on average compared to females.

title: North Dakota
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

text:
447
57%
4
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most prison sentences in New Hampshire include judicial minimum and maximum terms. Judicial minimum term may not exceed one-half of the maximum term.¹







In 2020, 57 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 43 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 7 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 89 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 50 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (21%) and property (20%) offenses.















In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 23 percent, changing from 2,761 in 2010 to 2,127 in 2022.

In 2020, 93 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 25 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses.















In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 24 percent, dropping from 1,556 in 2014 to 1,189 in 2020.









In 2020, 41 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 4 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 87 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

Race and EthnicitySex

In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 5.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New Hampshire.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New Hampshire.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.

The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where Hispanic people spent 8 more years in prison on average compared to Black people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1 year fewer on average in prison compared to males in New Hampshire.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 8.9 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 3.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New Hampshire.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New Hampshire.

All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 74 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Nebraska
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nebraska, prison sentences with maximum terms of four years or less have no parole release eligibility. Maximum terms of 20 years or more almost always include parole-release eligibility, with reductions for good time credits; this prison population becomes eligible at the 50-percent mark of the judicial maximum sentence or earlier.¹

title: Nebraska
text:
In 2020, 6 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 94 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 8 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 100 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 78 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 22 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (39%) and robbery (15%) offenses.















In 2020, 76 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between >=25 years.

title: Nebraska
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Missouri, minimum terms to parole-release eligibility for most people who are incarcerated are established by regulations by the parole board, with a range of 15–33 percent of the maximum sentence. By statute, dangerous offenses serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible, and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in increasing minimums of 40, 50, and 70 percent.¹







In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 32 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 5 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 66 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 34 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (25%) and robbery (15%) offenses.















In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 24 percent, changing from 30,623 in 2010 to 23,422 in 2022.

In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 22 percent, dropping from 19,511 in 2014 to 15,279 in 2020.









In 2020, 52 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 0 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 84 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

Race and EthnicitySex

Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.

The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Black people spent 4.9 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.

Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴
