title: Missouri
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Massachusetts, sentencing courts decide the length of minimum terms in relation to judicial maximum sentences. Credit-based deductions from both minimum and maximum terms are statutorily capped at 35 percent.¹







In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 59 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 38 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 96 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 47 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (29%) and rape or sexual assault (14%) offenses.















In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 100 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Prior to 2024 there were four tiers of eligibility formulas in Louisiana for different sentence classes with 25, 65, 75 and 100 percent categories.¹







In 2020, 61 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 39 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 9 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 100 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Unknown.










In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 57 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 43 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for robbery (23%) and public order (16%) offenses.















In 2020, 48 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 34 percent, changing from 39,445 in 2010 to 26,074 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 67 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 29 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 16 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for robbery offenses.















In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 23 percent, dropping from 18,335 in 2014 to 14,128 in 2020.









In 2020, 49 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 35 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 92 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 100 percent of people released from prison were Hispanic, any race.










In 2020, 90 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 34 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for drug offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 4.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Louisiana.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex










This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Hispanic people spent 0 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1.2 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Louisiana.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For robbery offenses, males spent 4.3 more years in prison on average compared to females.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, “National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.[pending]

title: Kentucky
text:
8,398
62%

People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Kentucky, the parole board sets parole eligibility for many offenses by policy: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in eight years; maximum terms of 2–39 years are eligible at 20 percent of the maximum sentence; and maximum terms of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at four months. By statute, nonviolent Class D offenses are eligible after serving 15 percent of the maximum and violent offenses are eligible at 85 percent.¹







In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 37 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 22 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 73 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 50 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (20%) and property (19%) offenses.















In 2020, 47 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (20 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 10 percent, changing from 20,544 in 2010 to 18,560 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 75 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 88 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 24 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drug offenses.















In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 6 percent, dropping from 18,024 in 2014 to 16,868 in 2020.









In 2020, 57 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 1 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 72 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 83 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 80 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 36 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 40 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for drug offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Kentucky.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 0.8 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.2 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent 2.7 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.6 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Kentucky.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For rape or sexual assault offenses, males spent 2.7 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 3.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Kentucky.














Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent an average of 0.6 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Kentucky, the parole board sets parole eligibility for many offenses by policy: A maximum sentence of 40 years or more is eligible in eight years; maximum terms of 2–39 years are eligible at 20 percent of the maximum sentence; and maximum terms of one year and up to but not including two years are eligible at four months. By statute, nonviolent Class D offenses are eligible after serving 15 percent of the maximum and violent offenses are eligible at 85 percent.¹

title: Kentucky
text:
In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 37 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 22 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 73 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 50 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (20%) and property (19%) offenses.















In 2020, 47 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: Kentucky
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (20 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent 2.7 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.

title: Kentucky
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Georgia, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences, with some exceptions.¹







In 2020, 76 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 23 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 14 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 60 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 69 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 31 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (21%) and robbery (20%) offenses.















In 2020, 59 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Arkansas, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for parole release—called “transfer to community correction”—after serving minimum terms of one-third or one-half of their judicial maximum sentences, which may be reduced by “meritorious good time” credits. Sorting into the one-third and one-half categories is based on the seriousness determination made by the Sentencing Commission. Substantial changes in release law will take effect in 2025 under Act 659 (2023).¹







In 2020, 8 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 29 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 49 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 49 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 51 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (34%) and property (26%) offenses.















In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















ATTENTION, ERROR: This state should not be in the toolTo match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent for most people, and 70 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 5 percent, changing from 16,204 in 2010 to 17,022 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 54 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 21 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drug offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 34 percent, dropping from 22,078 in 2014 to 29,674 in 2020.









In 2020, 78 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 0 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 44 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 70 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 84 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 36 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 43 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for drug offenses.

Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.

In 2020, Black people spent 0.7 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 1.4 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where people of Other race(s) people spent 3.7 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.4 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Arkansas.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 2.6 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 4.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Arkansas.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: National Snapshot
text: While the most recent available data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) is from 2020, we’ve projected the numbers for 2023 to offer a more current view of how many people are in prison beyond their parole eligibility and how this impacts prison populations.

title: National Snapshot
text:
Parole Eligibility Data by State
This table provides a detailed projection of the 2023 snapshot of state-level prison populations, including the percentage and number of people who are incarcerated beyond their parole eligibility. It also highlights the number of parole board members for each state.







*The number of parole board members includes appointed members and does not include subordinate hearing examiners that many states employ.



 Download Table

title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Alabama, sentences of five years or less have no minimum term to parole-release eligibility. Sentences of more than five and up to 10 years have an initial parole consideration approximately 18 months prior to the minimum release date. Sentences of more than 10 and up to 15 years have an initial parole consideration 30 months prior to the minimum release date.¹







In 2020, 0 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 42 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 0 percent.

In 2020, 44 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 1 to 1.9 years.

title: Alabama
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Alabama, sentences of five years or less have no minimum term to parole-release eligibility. Sentences of more than five and up to 10 years have an initial parole consideration approximately 18 months prior to the minimum release date. Sentences of more than 10 and up to 15 years have an initial parole consideration 30 months prior to the minimum release date.¹

title: Alabama
text:
In 2020, 0 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 42 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 0 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 97 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 44 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 14 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 86 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for public order (34%) and drug (31%) offenses.















In 2020, 44 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 1 to 1.9 years.

title: Alabama
text: ATTENTION, ERROR: This state should not be in the toolTo match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Alabama
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

text:
2,994

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most felonies in Colorado, people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court. This eligibility formula assumes that a person in prison has earned all available good time credits of 15 days per month.¹







In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 72 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 19 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 46 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 48 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 52 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (24%) and public order (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 47 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 30 percent, changing from 22,815 in 2010 to 15,865 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge

In 2020, 19 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault offenses.















In 2020, 23 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 3 percent, dropping from 9,884 in 2014 to 9,632 in 2020.









In 2020, 67 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 14 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 88 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

title: Iowa
text:
5,612
100%
5
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Generally, prison sentences in Iowa include a judicial maximum term but no minimum term. Most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release immediately upon admission.¹







In 2020, 100 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility stayed the same.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 64 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 46 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 54 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (25%) and drug (16%) offenses.















In 2020, 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 100 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 9 percent, changing from 9,455 in 2010 to 8,562 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 64 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 23 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for property offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 2 percent, dropping from 5,268 in 2014 to 5,181 in 2020.









In 2020, 36 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 2 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 78 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 70 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 86 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 37 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 35 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for property offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 9.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Iowa.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Iowa.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 0.3 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.2 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where Black people spent 7.1 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.7 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Iowa.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For rape or sexual assault offenses, males spent 5.4 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 9.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Iowa.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.8 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Iowa.














Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent an average of 0.4 more years in prison past parole eligibility and Hispanic people spent an average of 0.2 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

Avg. Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility 



2.2 Years


1.9 Years


1.8 Years


1.4 Years


Black


Hispanic


White


Other



This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity observed was for rape or sexual assault offenses, where people of Black, non-Hispanic had 5.4 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to people of Other race(s) people, who had the shortest time in prison past parole eligibility for these offenses.










Females spent an average of 0.8 fewer years in prison past parole eligibility compared to males.

Avg. Years in Prison Past Parole Eligibility 



2 Years


1.1 Years


Male


Female



This chart shows the average years in prison past parole eligibility for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 4.1 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to females.


















While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.









¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Iowa (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, December 2020), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/iowa_doi_report_3_3_21.pdf.² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., “National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.³ Kevin Reitz et al., “American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report” (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, pp. 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, “National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.[pending]
Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program.

text:

People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Maryland, people who are convicted of nonviolent crimes with sentences of more than six months become eligible for parole after serving one-fourth of the maximum sentence. People in prison convicted of a “violent crime” become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum.¹







In 2020, 55 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 45 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 0 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 67 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 97 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 77 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 23 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (21%) and robbery (20%) offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 33 percent, changing from 22,645 in 2010 to 15,134 in 2022.

In 2020, 96 percent of people in prison were male.

In 2020, 25 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses.















In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2016 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 32 percent, dropping from 8,944 in 2016 to 6,048 in 2020.









In 2020, 37 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 37 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 79 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.
