title: Maryland
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Maryland, people who are convicted of nonviolent crimes with sentences of more than six months become eligible for parole after serving one-fourth of the maximum sentence. People in prison convicted of a “violent crime” become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum.¹

title: Maryland
text:
In 2020, 55 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 45 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 0 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 67 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 97 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 77 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 23 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (21%) and robbery (20%) offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: Maryland
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

text:

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Mississippi, under the general rules for people in prison convicted of nonviolent crimes, parole-release eligibility occurs at the 25-percent mark of their judicial maximum terms or at 10 years, whichever is less. For most prisoners convicted of violent offenses, parole-release eligibility comes at the 50-percent milestone of their maximum terms or at 20 years, whichever is less.¹







In 2020, 59 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 41 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 15 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 65 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 63 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 37 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for robbery (24%) and rape or sexual assault (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

In 2020, 40 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 34 percent, dropping from 11,270 in 2014 to 7,463 in 2020.









In 2020, 34 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 32 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 92 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

title: Mississippi
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Montana
text:
908
54%
5
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most people who are incarcerated in Montana, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of their judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people who are incarcerated will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a few states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.¹







In 2020, 54 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 46 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 77 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 71 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 29 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (35%) and aggravated or simple assault (19%) offenses.















In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 100 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 16 percent, changing from 3,716 in 2010 to 4,313 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 71 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 86 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 27 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 2 percent, dropping from 1,126 in 2014 to 1,144 in 2020.









In 2020, 45 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 3 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 75 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 67 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 87 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 20 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for drug offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 7.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Montana.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Montana.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.

The largest disparity was observed among other violent offenses offenses, where Black people spent 2.6 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.7 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Montana.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, females spent 3.4 more years in prison on average compared to males.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nevada, for each of five felony grades, the court sets a minimum term that can be no less than one year and no more than 40 percent of the judicial maximum sentence. Nevada has a multilayered system of good time and earned time credits, which are deducted from the minimum and maximum terms. The advancement of parole-release eligibility is limited to 58 percent of the minimum term.¹







In 2020, 46 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 54 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 1 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 59 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for public order (29%) and property (22%) offenses.















In 2020, 42 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (40 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 40 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Nevada
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nevada, for each of five felony grades, the court sets a minimum term that can be no less than one year and no more than 40 percent of the judicial maximum sentence. Nevada has a multilayered system of good time and earned time credits, which are deducted from the minimum and maximum terms. The advancement of parole-release eligibility is limited to 58 percent of the minimum term.¹

title: Nevada
text:
In 2020, 46 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 54 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 1 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 59 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for public order (29%) and property (22%) offenses.















In 2020, 42 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: Nevada
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (40 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 40 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

text:

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In New York, most people who are incarcerated are serving “determinate” sentences, but a substantial number are serving “indeterminate” sentences that receive a judicial minimum and maximum, with no statutory constraint on the ratio between them. People serving indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole “at any time” after the expiration of their minimum sentences.¹







In 2020, 68 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 32 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 13 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 97 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (16%) and public order (16%) offenses.















In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 46 percent, changing from 56,656 in 2010 to 30,338 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 50 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 96 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 21 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses.















In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 28 percent, dropping from 22,506 in 2014 to 16,216 in 2020.









In 2020, 42 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 2 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 93 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 44 percent of people released from prison were Black, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 93 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 37 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 26 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for property offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 8.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New York.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New York.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex


In 2020, Black people spent 0.8 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.7 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among negligent manslaughter offenses, where Hispanic people spent 2.6 more years in prison on average compared to White people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1.2 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in New York.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 6.1 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 9.6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New York.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 3.4 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in New York.

The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: New York
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
The Oklahoma parole board has authority to grant discretionary parole release only to prisoners convicted of nonviolent offenses. For prisoners convicted of “violent offenses,” the parole board is limited to making recommendations to release to the governor. For offenses committed between July 1, 1998, and November 1, 2018, people who are incarcerated become eligible after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. For offenses committed on or after November 1, 2018, people become eligible after one-fourth of their judicial maximum terms. People convicted of violent offenses become eligible after 85 percent of their maximum.¹







In 2020, 68 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 32 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 21 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 76 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Unknown.










In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old.


















In 2020, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 38 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (20%) and property (16%) offenses.















In 2020, 54 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 7 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 98 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 66 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 34 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (22%) and aggravated or simple assault (17%) offenses.















In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 33 percent, changing from 3,357 in 2010 to 2,238 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 38 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 95 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 32 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 17 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses.















In 2020, 21 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 36 percent, dropping from 3,592 in 2014 to 2,293 in 2020.









In 2020, 68 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 2 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 11 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 49 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 39 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 29 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for public order offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 11.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Rhode Island.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Rhode Island.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.

In 2020, Black people spent 0.3 more years on average in prison, and Hispanic people spent 0.1 more years on average in prison compared to White people.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where Black people spent 4.5 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 0.6 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in Rhode Island.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 4.8 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 12.4 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Rhode Island.









In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 3.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Rhode Island.

In 2020, Black people spent an average of 0.4 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people.

The largest disparity observed was for murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where people of Black, non-Hispanic had 13.3 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to people of White, non-Hispanic people, who had the shortest time in prison past parole eligibility for these offenses.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

In Rhode Island, most people who are incarcerated with sentences of more than six months become eligible for discretionary parole release when they have served one-third of their judicial maximum sentences.¹

text:

*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Dakota, by statute there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from the 25- to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. For crimes committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made nonparolable. A large percentage of people who are incarcerated are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission, with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.”¹







In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 63 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 20 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge




Data is not available. South Dakota did not submit this data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2020.




In 2020, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 66 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 34 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (26%) and aggravated or simple assault (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 2 percent, changing from 3,434 in 2010 to 3,353 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge

In 2020, 85 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.

In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison increased 36 percent, dropping from 2,156 in 2014 to 2,929 in 2020.









In 2020, 68 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 50 percent increase compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 100 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

title: South Dakota
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Dakota, by statute there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from the 25- to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. For crimes committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made nonparolable. A large percentage of people who are incarcerated are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission, with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.”¹

title: South Dakota
text:
In 2020, 37 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 63 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 20 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge




Data is not available. South Dakota did not submit this data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2020.




In 2020, 91 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 66 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 34 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (26%) and aggravated or simple assault (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

title: South Dakota
text: Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: South Dakota
text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Texas, people with lower-severity offenses become eligible for parole when their time served plus good time credits equals one-quarter of the maximum sentence or 15 years, whichever is less. People with higher-severity offenses become eligible for parole at one-half of the maximum sentence or 30 years, whichever is less, and without reduction by good time.¹







In 2020, 67 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 33 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 1 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Hispanic, any race.










In 2020, 93 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 63 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 37 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (19%) and robbery (18%) offenses.















In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 23 percent, changing from 173,649 in 2010 to 133,772 in 2022.

title: West Virginia
text:
2,376
43%
9
People In Prison PastParole Eligibility*(2023 Projection)
Prison Population IncarceratedPast Parole Eligibility(2023 Projection)
Parole BoardMembers





*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In West Virginia, indeterminate sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. “Definite sentences” include terms to parole-release eligibility that are set by statutory formula at 25 percent.¹







In 2020, 43 percent of people in prison were past their parole eligibility. Another 57 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2020.








From 2014 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 26 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 88 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.










In 2020, 38 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 38 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 62 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (34%) and public order (15%) offenses.















In 2020, 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
















Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴





From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 12 percent, changing from 6,681 in 2010 to 5,847 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 85 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 90 percent of people in prison were male.










In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 23 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for property offenses.















In 2020, 40 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2014 to 2020, people released from prison decreased 6 percent, dropping from 3,565 in 2014 to 3,342 in 2020.









In 2020, 41 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 23 percent decrease compared to 2014.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 70 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.
















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, 87 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic.










In 2020, 84 percent of people released from prison were male.










In 2020, 39 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 38 percent of people released from prison were for people incarcerated for property offenses.














Race and EthnicitySex


Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.










In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.


Sex disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.












Race and EthnicitySex










This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Hispanic people spent 4 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses.










In 2020, females spent 1.2 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in West Virginia.








This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by sex in 2020. For murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 6.8 more years in prison on average compared to females.


















Race and EthnicitySex












In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 2.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in West Virginia.














Race and EthnicitySex


No disparities compared to White people found for West Virginia.
